1: wayyirpu yāḏāyw: ‘his hands became feeble/slack’ — the idiom for loss of courage and fighting capacity (Jeremiah 38:4; Ezekiel 21:7; Isaiah 35:3). Ishbosheth’s collapse on hearing of Abner’s death shows the puppet king’s total dependence on Abner’s military and political support. ‘All Israel was dismayed’ (niḇhălû, H926, terrified/confused) — the same root used for the terror of enemies before YHWH’s action. The house of Saul’s final disintegration begins with a single piece of news about its last pillar.
2: The two commanders (śārê gĕḏûḏîm, commanders of raiding parties) Baanah and Rechab are identified with precision — named, patronymized, and tribally located. The sons of Rimmon the Beerothite: Beeroth was a Gibeonite city (Joshua 9:17) incorporated into Benjamin, whose inhabitants were driven out and became resident aliens. The political instability of the Benjaminite fringe — non-native commanders serving a weakening king — sets up their murderous calculation.
3: The parenthetical note that the Beerothites ‘fled to Gittaim and lived there as resident foreigners (gērîm) to this day’ explains a displacement event and signals the Beerothites’ liminal status. Gēr (H1616, resident alien/sojourner) carried specific legal protections in Israelite law (Exodus 22:21; Leviticus 19:34; Deuteronomy 24:17-18) — YHWH identifies with the gēr as one he specially protects. The narrator’s ‘to this day’ (ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh) is an etiological marker connecting a historical event to a still-visible state of affairs in the narrator’s own time.
4: The narrative pauses for Mephibosheth: Jonathan’s son, lame in both feet, five years old when the news of Jezreel came. This parenthetical is inserted at the collapse of Saul’s house because Mephibosheth is the sole surviving heir — and David’s covenant with Jonathan (1 Samuel 20:15-16) requires his protection when the rival dynasty falls. Mĕpîḇōšeṯ (alternate: mĕrîḇ-baʿal, 1 Chronicles 8:34 — baal is replaced with bōšeṯ, shame, in the anti-Baal name-editing of later tradition). His lameness is introduced here as the social vulnerability that makes David’s later covenant loyalty to him (2 Sam 9) the more significant act of grace.
5: The assassins arrive at Ishbosheth’s house ‘during the heat of the day’ (ḥōm hayyôm) — midday when activity stops and guards are slack (cf. 1 Kings 20:16; the same phrase in Genesis 18:1 for Abraham’s midday encounter with the three visitors). The tactic of arriving under the pretense of bringing wheat (ḥiṭṭîm) exploits the normal agricultural commerce that would gain admission to a household compound. The weaponized ordinary transaction is the assassination technique — using routine economic activity as cover for lethal intent.
6: They slip into the interior of the house under the wheat-gathering pretense and stab him in the belly (hammĕṣ, the fifth, i.e., the abdomen). The intimate, indoor assassination of the king on his midday rest is the extreme violation of both hospitality and royal protection protocols. The detailed narration emphasizes the premeditation and treachery — facts David will cite in his judgment speech. The anatomy-specific murder (belly wound) contrasts with the honorable battlefield deaths the narrative otherwise records.
7: They struck him (wayyakkuhû waymîṯuhû wĕyāqōzzû ʾet-rōʾšô): killed him and cut off his head — and traveled through the Arabah all night, the route from Transjordan to Hebron. The beheading of the enemy king and transport of the head to the victor follows ANE military conventions (cf. 1 Samuel 17:51; 2 Samuel 31:9-10 — both involving Saul’s house). They calculate that David will reward them for eliminating his rival; instead he will execute them for the murder of YHWH’s anointed.
8: “Here is the head of Ishbosheth son of Saul, your enemy, who sought your life. Today YHWH has given my lord the king vengeance on Saul and his offspring.” The assassins frame their murder as YHWH’s action — a theodicy of political violence. The claim that YHWH executed vengeance through their act is the self-serving theological interpretation that David will explicitly reject. The verb nāqam (H5358, to avenge/take vengeance) — David has never sought vengeance against Saul’s house; the killers project onto David’s motives what was entirely their own calculation.
9: David’s oath formula: ḥay YHWH ʾăšer pāḏāh ʾet-napšî mikol-ṣārāh — ‘As YHWH lives, who has redeemed (pāḏāh, H6299) my life from every affliction.’ The verb pāḏāh (to ransom/redeem by payment) — used for the ransom of the firstborn (Exodus 13:13-15; Numbers 18:15-16), and for YHWH’s redemption of Israel from Egypt (Deuteronomy 7:8; 2 Samuel 9:26; 2 Samuel 13:5; 2 Samuel 7:23). David grounds his oath in YHWH as the one who has been his personal redeemer — the theological attribution that gives the following judgment its divine authority.
10: David cites the precedent: the Amalekite at Ziklag (2 Samuel 1:1-16) who reported Saul’s death, thinking he was bringing ‘good news’ (mĕḇaśśēr, H1319 — to announce glad tidings, the root of bĕśôrāh/gospel). David had the man executed despite his claim that Saul had asked to be killed. The legal precedent David invokes: even if Saul deserved death, the killer of YHWH’s anointed is not rewarded. The pattern of David refusing to receive the anointed’s death as ‘good news’ from those who caused it runs consistently through 2 Samuel 1-4.
11: ʾanāšîm rĕšāʿîm ʾăšer hārĕgû ʾet-ʾîš ṣaddîq bĕḇêṯô ʿal-miškāḇô — ‘wicked men who murdered a righteous man in his own house on his bed.’ The contrast between rāšāʿ (wicked) and ṣaddîq (righteous) in David’s verdict identifies the murder in covenantal terms: Ishbosheth, however politically weak, was a ‘righteous man’ (ṣaddîq) in the sense of having done nothing deserving death. The question ‘should I not now require his blood from your hands?’ (hălōʾ ʾăḇaqqēš ʾet-dāmô miyyedĕkem) uses bāqaš (to seek/require) — YHWH requires blood-guilt from those who shed innocent blood (Genesis 9:5; 2 Samuel 42:22).
12: David orders their execution and the cutting of their hands and feet — the body parts that acted in the murder — and the hanging of their bodies at the pool in Hebron. The mutilation of the executioners’ hands (which killed) and feet (which carried them to Hebron) is the lex talionis principle applied with specificity. The public display at the pool of Hebron — David’s capital — serves the same justice-signaling function as the public display of Saul’s body at Beth-Shan (1 Samuel 31:10): covenant justice visibly executed prevents the perception of impunity.
